facts.text: The A-Team's Mr. T wore so much real gold jewelry on set that the wardrobe department eventually had to weigh it before each shoot.
Despite constant gunfire and explosions, no one was ever killed in The A-Team — a rule that became a running joke among fans.
Mr. T was cast in The A-Team after Sylvester Stallone spotted him in a televised tough-man competition.
George Peppard, who played Hannibal, reportedly didn't get along well with Mr. T on set throughout the series.
KITT in Knight Rider was built on a Pontiac Trans Am chassis — a choice partly driven by GM's sponsorship of the show.
The voice of KITT was provided by William Daniels, who also played Mr. Feeny on Boy Meets World over a decade later.
Multiple KITT cars were built for Knight Rider — one for stunts, one for close-ups, and several backup vehicles.
David Hasselhoff's contract for Knight Rider gave him final approval over storylines involving his character Michael Knight.
Tom Selleck almost didn't star in Magnum P.I. — he was originally cast as Indiana Jones but couldn't leave the show.
Magnum P.I. was set in Hawaii and the production team used real locations around Oahu for most exterior shots.
Tom Selleck's mustache became so iconic during Magnum P.I. that he was reportedly asked to keep it for every project that followed.
Magnum P.I. was one of the first prime-time shows to regularly feature a Vietnam veteran as a main character.
Cheers was filmed entirely at Paramount Studios in Hollywood — the bar set never moved for the show's entire 11-season run.
The Cheers theme song 'Where Everybody Knows Your Name' was written by Gary Portnoy and Judy Hart Angelo.
Cheers debuted in 1982 with very low ratings and nearly got cancelled after its first season before building a loyal audience.
The bar used for exterior shots in Cheers was the Bull & Finch Pub in Boston, which later renamed itself 'Cheers.'
Michael J. Fox almost wasn't cast in Family Ties — the producers originally wanted a different actor for the role of Alex P. Keaton.
Family Ties reversed the intended dynamic — Alex was supposed to be a minor character, but Fox's performance made him the fan favorite.
Michael J. Fox filmed Back to the Future while appearing in Family Ties, working double shifts for months.
Meredith Baxter and Michael Gross, who played the Keaton parents, both went on to have successful post-Family Ties careers.
Diff'rent Strokes launched Gary Coleman into superstardom — his catchphrase 'Whatchu talkin' 'bout, Willis?' became one of the most repeated lines of the 1980s.
Three of Diff'rent Strokes' main child stars faced serious legal troubles in their adult years, prompting discussions about child star welfare in Hollywood.
Diff'rent Strokes was one of the first American sitcoms to feature a primarily Black cast adopted into a wealthy white family.
The Cosby Show is credited with reviving the sitcom format in the mid-1980s after the genre had fallen out of favor with networks.
The Cosby Show was filmed before a live studio audience in New York rather than in Hollywood, which was unusual for the time.
The Huxtables' home on The Cosby Show was portrayed as a Brooklyn brownstone, adding a realistic, grounded setting to the affluent family.
Leonardo DiCaprio joined Growing Pains in its sixth season and was so outstanding that he attracted attention from Hollywood directors.
Growing Pains was one of several 1980s family sitcoms that reversed the traditional family dynamic by making the father the stay-at-home parent.
Kirk Cameron, star of Growing Pains, became a born-again Christian during the show's run, influencing storylines in later seasons.
ALF — Alien Life Form — was performed using a combination of a puppet, a costume, and camera angles to keep the mechanism hidden.
The ALF puppet required up to five operators to control simultaneously for complex scenes involving both movement and speech.
ALF's catchphrase 'Ha! I kill me!' was voted one of the most memorable comedy catchphrases of the 1980s.
ALF stood for Alien Life Form — the character's actual name on his home planet Melmac was Gordon Shumway.
MacGyver's producers employed technical advisors to ensure the improvised gadgets shown were at least theoretically plausible.
The word 'MacGyver' entered the Merriam-Webster dictionary as a verb, meaning to fix or make something using whatever materials are available.
Richard Dean Anderson, who played MacGyver, was a hockey player before turning to acting — he missed the NHL draft by one spot.
MacGyver famously never carried a gun, a character choice that made the show stand out from other action series of the 1980s.
Miami Vice was developed from a two-word pitch — 'MTV Cops' — submitted by writer Anthony Yerkovich.
Miami Vice is credited with popularizing the pastel blazer and no-socks look that defined 1980s men's fashion.
Jan Hammer composed Miami Vice's electronic score, winning an Emmy and making the synthesizer-driven soundtrack as iconic as the visuals.
Miami Vice used real luxury cars, yachts, and high-end fashion to give the show a visual authenticity that most TV productions avoided.
Airwolf's helicopter was a real Bell 222 modified with fake weapons pods and aerodynamic add-ons for the show.
The Airwolf helicopter was later sold to a German air ambulance service after the show ended.
Jan-Michael Vincent, who starred in Airwolf, was reportedly one of the highest-paid TV actors of the early 1980s.
The Dukes of Hazzard went through 309 General Lee Dodge Chargers during its run — most were destroyed in jumps and stunts.
The General Lee's horn played the first 12 notes of 'Dixie,' which became inseparable from the show's identity.
John Schneider lied about his age to get cast in The Dukes of Hazzard — he said he was 24 when he was actually 18.
Simon & Simon ran for eight seasons on CBS and became one of the highest-rated detective dramas of the early 1980s.
Simon & Simon featured two brothers with opposite personalities as private detectives — a setup that influenced many later buddy shows.
The 'Who shot J.R.?' cliffhanger from Dallas in 1980 remains one of the most watched single episodes in American television history.
The answer to 'Who shot J.R.?' in Dallas was kept secret from almost the entire cast and crew until the episode aired.
Dallas spawned numerous international adaptations and is credited with popularizing the nighttime soap opera format worldwide.
Dynasty was created specifically to compete with Dallas and quickly surpassed it in the ratings by the mid-1980s.
Joan Collins was cast in Dynasty after the producers saw her in a TV movie and decided to rewrite the show around a stronger female villain.
Dynasty's Alexis Carrington, played by Joan Collins, became one of the most iconic TV villains of the 1980s.
The Facts of Life is a spinoff of Diff'rent Strokes — Mrs. Garrett moved from the Drummond household to Eastland School.
The Facts of Life started with a large ensemble cast that was trimmed to four main girls after the first season.
Saved by the Bell evolved from a Saturday morning show called Good Morning, Miss Bliss, which was originally set in Indiana.
Mark-Paul Gosselaar, who played Zack Morris, wore a hair piece in some episodes because his natural hair wouldn't hold the right style.
Dustin Diamond, who played Screech in Saved by the Bell, wrote a controversial memoir that other cast members publicly disputed.
Star Trek: TNG debuted in 1987 as a first-run syndicated show rather than on a broadcast network — unusual for a major sci-fi series.
Patrick Stewart was initially reluctant to shave his head for Captain Picard, but Gene Roddenberry insisted baldness would be unremarkable by the 24th century.
Star Trek: TNG's episode 'The Best of Both Worlds' Part 1 cliffhanger caused a spike in TV sales when it aired in 1990.
Whoopi Goldberg personally lobbied to appear on Star Trek: TNG because she credited the original series' Uhura with inspiring her career.
Married... with Children was the first original scripted series to air on the Fox network when it launched in 1987.
A viewer's protest letter campaign against Married... with Children inadvertently boosted the show's ratings by drawing attention to it.
Ed O'Neill, who played Al Bundy, had a brief tryout with the Pittsburgh Steelers before becoming an actor.
The Wonder Years was narrated by Daniel Stern but the voice was presented as an older version of Kevin Arnold looking back on his childhood.
The Wonder Years won the Emmy for Outstanding Comedy Series in 1988, its first year on air.
Joe Cocker's cover of 'With a Little Help from My Friends' that opened The Wonder Years became one of the most recognized theme songs of the era.
Roseanne debuted in 1988 and immediately became one of the highest-rated shows on television, staying in the top five for most of its run.
Roseanne was groundbreaking for depicting a working-class American family at a time when most sitcoms featured comfortable middle-class households.
The fictional Conner family in Roseanne lived in Lanford, Illinois — a setting chosen to contrast with the glamorous settings of other sitcoms.
Full House debuted in 1987 and was originally expected to be cancelled quickly, but became one of ABC's highest-rated shows.
The Olsen twins, Mary-Kate and Ashley, shared the role of Michelle Tanner in Full House because of child labor laws limiting filming hours.
Full House was set in San Francisco and used the iconic Painted Ladies Victorian row houses in its opening credits.
Quantum Leap's premise — leaping into other people's bodies through time — was created after networks rejected more conventional time travel pitches.
Scott Bakula performed many of his own stunts on Quantum Leap, particularly in episodes where Sam leaped into athletes or performers.
The Quantum Leap finale remains one of the most controversial series endings, as it revealed Sam never returned home.
Moonlighting is credited with helping launch Bruce Willis's career — he was a relatively unknown actor before the show debuted in 1985.
Moonlighting regularly broke the fourth wall and used experimental formats, including a black-and-white episode and a Shakespeare episode.
The romantic tension between David and Maddie in Moonlighting gave rise to the term 'Moonlighting Curse' for shows that resolve romantic tension too soon.
Edward Woodward played Robert McCall in The Equalizer, a former intelligence agent who helps ordinary people facing impossible situations.
The Equalizer's New York City setting was filmed almost entirely on location, giving it a grittier, more realistic atmosphere than studio-shot shows.
Pierce Brosnan was unable to accept the James Bond role for GoldenEye initially because his Remington Steele contract was extended unexpectedly.
Remington Steele reversed the usual TV detective formula by having the female character be the actual competent detective and the male the charming front.
Hart to Hart starred Robert Wagner and Stefanie Powers as a wealthy couple who stumble into mysteries — loosely inspired by Nick and Nora Charles.
The Hart to Hart theme narrated by Lionel Stander became one of the most parodied TV openings of the early 1980s.
The Fall Guy starred Lee Majors as a Hollywood stuntman who moonlighted as a bounty hunter — a premise based loosely on real stunt culture.
The Fall Guy's theme 'The Unknown Stuntman' was performed by Lee Majors himself and reached the country music charts.
Scarecrow and Mrs. King starred Kate Jackson and Bruce Boxleitner as a spy and ordinary housewife team — a combination that was unusual for action shows.
Trapper John, M.D. was a sequel series set 28 years after M*A*S*H, featuring Pernell Roberts as a much older Trapper John.
St. Elsewhere was one of the first TV dramas to kill off a main character — Dr. White — midway through a season.
The famous St. Elsewhere finale revealed the entire series had taken place in the imagination of an autistic boy looking at a snow globe.
Hill Street Blues pioneered the serialized drama format on American television, with storylines carrying over between episodes.
Hill Street Blues won eight Emmy Awards in its first season in 1981, including Outstanding Drama Series.
Hill Street Blues' iconic opening scene — the morning roll call — became a template copied by countless police procedurals.
Cagney & Lacey was the first US drama to star two women as equals in the lead roles — not as sidekicks or love interests.
Cagney & Lacey was cancelled after its first season but fan letter campaigns convinced CBS to renew it, and it ran for six more seasons.
Murder, She Wrote ran for 12 seasons and made Angela Lansbury one of the highest-paid actors in television history.
Cabot Cove, Maine — the fictional town in Murder, She Wrote — would statistically have the world's highest per-capita murder rate.
Angela Lansbury was initially reluctant to take the role of Jessica Fletcher, worrying it would typecast her as an elderly amateur detective.
Bill Bixby, who played David Banner in The Incredible Hulk, requested that the character's first name be changed from Bruce to avoid gay stereotyping, according to producers.
Lou Ferrigno, who played the Hulk, was genuinely deaf and worked with a sign language interpreter on set.
The Incredible Hulk's melancholy closing theme became so associated with sad departures that it was widely used in parody.
The Six Million Dollar Man's bionic sound effects — a slow electronic pulse — were created by simply slowing down a recording and adding a wobble effect.
The Bionic Woman spinoff was so popular it temporarily outrated the show it spun off from, The Six Million Dollar Man.
Lynda Carter performed many of her own stunts in Wonder Woman, including several involving the golden lasso.
Wonder Woman's first season was set in World War II, but was moved to the modern day for season two to reduce production costs.
21 Jump Street launched Johnny Depp's career — he was cast as Officer Hanson when the show struggled to find a lead actor.
21 Jump Street was one of the first shows on the fledgling Fox network to become a genuine hit.
Johnny Depp reportedly grew frustrated with his teen idol status from 21 Jump Street and actively sought edgier film roles to escape it.
The Love Boat was filmed almost entirely aboard actual Pacific Princess cruise ships, which saw bookings increase significantly during the show's run.
The Love Boat featured hundreds of guest stars across its nine seasons, making it one of the most celebrity-packed shows in TV history.
Ricardo Montalbán's white suit in Fantasy Island became one of the most iconic TV costumes of the late 1970s and early 1980s.
Hervé Villechaize, who played Tattoo in Fantasy Island, reportedly had contract disputes over pay that contributed to his departure from the show.
WKRP in Cincinnati's Thanksgiving turkey-drop episode is consistently ranked among the greatest single episodes in sitcom history.
The WKRP theme song's outro contained deliberately unintelligible lyrics — a joke that creator Hugh Wilson wrote as gibberish on purpose.
The M*A*S*H finale in 1983 drew 106 million viewers — a record for a series finale that stood for 27 years.
M*A*S*H ran for 11 seasons, nearly four times longer than the actual Korean War it depicted.
Barney Miller was filmed almost entirely on a single precinct set, making it one of the most cost-efficient quality dramas in TV history.
Barney Miller was praised for portraying police officers as complex, overworked, and often frustrated by bureaucracy — rare for cop shows.
Three's Company was based on a British sitcom called Man About the House, purchased and adapted for American audiences.
John Ritter's physical comedy in Three's Company was so precise that critics compared it favorably to classic silent film performers.
The Golden Girls debuted in 1985 and consistently ranked in the top 10 for all seven of its seasons.
All four main Golden Girls actresses won Emmy Awards for their roles — the only cast in sitcom history to accomplish this.
The Golden Girls was set in Miami specifically to justify the characters' warm-weather lifestyle and outdoor scenes.
Bea Arthur, Betty White, Rue McClanahan, and Estelle Getty were all in their late 50s to early 60s when Golden Girls began.
Perfect Strangers debuted Bronson Pinchot's Balki Bartokomous character, one of the most beloved immigrant comedy roles of the 1980s.
Perfect Strangers was set in Chicago and used real city locations for exterior shots across its eight-season run.
The Newhart finale in 1990 revealed the entire series had been a dream of Bob Hartley from The Bob Newhart Show — widely considered one of TV's greatest endings.
Bob Newhart's deadpan telephone conversation style, honed on the earlier Bob Newhart Show, carried directly into the Newhart format.
A Different World was a spinoff of The Cosby Show focusing on Denise Huxtable at a fictional historically Black college.
Night Court was set in a real New York City night court and consultants helped the writers accurately depict after-hours legal proceedings.
John Larroquette won four consecutive Emmy Awards for Supporting Actor for his role as Dan Fielding in Night Court.
Designing Women was set in Atlanta and focused on a women-run interior design firm, exploring social issues more directly than most sitcoms.
L.A. Law won the Emmy for Outstanding Drama Series four times and is credited with launching a wave of legal dramas.
L.A. Law tackled then-controversial subjects including AIDS, sexual harassment, and racial discrimination in its storylines.
thirtysomething deliberately used a lowercase title to evoke the informal, conversational tone the show aimed for.
thirtysomething was groundbreaking for featuring baby boomers grappling with middle-class adult anxieties rather than dramatic crises.
Tour of Duty was one of the first American TV series to tackle the Vietnam War as its primary setting.
China Beach portrayed the Vietnam War from the perspective of military nurses and women — a virtually unexplored angle in previous Vietnam media.
Northern Exposure, which debuted in 1990, used location shooting in Roslyn, Washington to portray the fictional Alaskan town of Cicely.
In the Heat of the Night starred Carroll O'Connor as a Southern police chief and was praised for its nuanced treatment of race relations.
Spenser: For Hire was based on Robert B. Parker's popular detective novels and filmed on location in Boston.
Michael Landon created, produced, wrote, and starred in Highway to Heaven simultaneously — an unusual level of control for a TV actor.
Highway to Heaven featured a different story each week with Landon and Victor French as an angel and his human companion helping people.
Little House on the Prairie ended its run in 1983 with a finale episode where the town of Walnut Grove was literally blown up.
Mork & Mindy began as a spinoff of Happy Days after Robin Williams' guest appearance as Mork from Ork became a sensation.
Robin Williams improvised so frequently on Mork & Mindy that scripts often had blank spaces marked 'Mork does stuff here.'
The Fonz 'jumping the shark' on Happy Days gave rise to the phrase 'jumping the shark' to describe a show's creative decline.
Henry Winkler, who played Fonzie, holds a master's degree from Yale School of Drama despite his iconic role as a tough greaser.
Laverne & Shirley was a Happy Days spinoff that at one point became the most watched show on American television.
Penny Marshall, who played Laverne, later became one of the most successful female film directors in Hollywood.
The Jeffersons ran for 11 seasons and was the longest-running primarily Black sitcom in American TV history at the time of its cancellation.
The Jeffersons was cancelled abruptly in 1985 — cast members reportedly found out through the press rather than from the network.
One Day at a Time was one of the first sitcoms to regularly feature a divorced single mother as the lead character.
Taxi launched the careers of Andy Kaufman, Danny DeVito, and Christopher Lloyd simultaneously.
Andy Kaufman's Latka Gravas character in Taxi was largely improvised and sometimes made the other actors break character on camera.
SCTV launched the careers of John Candy, Martin Short, Catherine O'Hara, Rick Moranis, and Eugene Levy simultaneously.
SCTV was originally produced in Canada on a shoestring budget, which led to creative workarounds that made the show funnier.
Saturday Night Live almost didn't survive the 1980s — NBC nearly cancelled it after a disastrous 1980-81 season with an entirely new cast.
Eddie Murphy joined SNL at age 19 in 1980 and became the show's breakout star during a period when it was struggling for identity.
DuckTales debuted in 1987 and became one of the highest-rated animated shows in the history of syndicated television.
DuckTales was based on Carl Barks' Uncle Scrooge comic books, and creator Barks was consulted during the show's development.
The DuckTales theme song is one of the most recognized animated theme songs ever recorded and was voted by fans as the catchiest cartoon theme.
G.I. Joe: A Real American Hero animated every episode to end with a public service announcement — the origin of 'Knowing is half the battle.'
G.I. Joe was produced to accompany Hasbro's relaunched action figure line in 1982 and used many character names directly from the toys.
The Transformers animated series killed off Optimus Prime in the 1986 movie to introduce new toy characters — which caused significant backlash from children.
The Transformers cartoon was produced partly in Japan by Toei Animation, giving some episodes a notably different visual style.
Orson Welles voiced Unicron in the 1986 Transformers movie — it was one of his final recorded performances before his death.
He-Man and the Masters of the Universe debuted in 1983 as one of the first cartoons made primarily to sell a toy line.
At its peak, He-Man merchandise generated over $400 million in annual revenue, making it one of the most profitable toy franchises of the decade.
She-Ra: Princess of Power was created partly to give girls a He-Man equivalent character and expand the Masters of the Universe toy line.
ThunderCats was produced by Rankin/Bass and animated in Japan — the theme song and voice acting were all performed in the United States.
Lion-O in ThunderCats is technically a child in an adult body — his aging was accelerated when he spent his formative years in suspended animation.
SilverHawks was created by the same team as ThunderCats and used a nearly identical production process, airing in the same year.
Voltron was adapted from two different Japanese anime series — Golion and Dairugger XV — combined and redubbed for American audiences.
Robotech was created by adapting three unrelated Japanese anime series — Macross, Southern Cross, and Mospeada — into a single connected story.
Robotech's serialized storytelling and mature themes were unusual for an American animated show in 1985 and influenced a generation of animation fans.
Inspector Gadget debuted in 1983 and was one of the first animated series to use computer assistance for its animation process.
Don Adams, who voiced Inspector Gadget, based the character's bumbling style directly on his Maxwell Smart character from Get Smart.
The Smurfs became the highest-rated Saturday morning cartoon in the United States after its NBC debut in 1981.
Smurfette was originally created by the villain Gargamel to cause chaos among the Smurfs, a backstory simplified for the cartoon.
Scooby-Doo went through numerous format changes during the 1980s, including Scrappy-Doo being added in 1979 and dominating into the early decade.
Jim Henson created Fraggle Rock with a stated goal of ending conflict between nations — he believed children's programming could influence world peace.
Fraggle Rock was simultaneously produced in multiple countries with different wraparound segments tailored to each region.
The Muppet Show ran from 1976 to 1981 and was produced in the UK because no American network would initially pick it up.
Pee-wee's Playhouse debuted in 1986 and won 22 Emmy Awards across its five seasons — a record for a children's program.
Pee-wee's Playhouse featured a deliberately surreal aesthetic that was influenced by the art direction of 1950s children's programming.
Captain Planet and the Planeteers debuted in 1990 and was created with Ted Turner's explicit goal of making children care about environmental issues.
The Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles cartoon debuted in 1987 as a five-part miniseries and became a full series after enormous viewer response.
The original Ninja Turtles comic by Eastman and Laird was a dark parody of Frank Miller's Daredevil — the cartoon lightened the tone significantly.
Each Ninja Turtle was named after a Renaissance artist — Leonardo, Donatello, Raphael, and Michelangelo.
The Real Ghostbusters was titled 'Real' to distinguish it from a competing animated Ghostbusters series based on a 1975 live-action show.
The Real Ghostbusters consistently altered the lore from the films, creating entirely new mythologies around ghost types and containment.
Jem and the Holograms was produced by Hasbro alongside Sunbow Entertainment, the same team behind G.I. Joe and Transformers.
Jem featured completely original music for each episode — over 150 songs were written and produced for the series' run.
Danger Mouse was a British animated series that parodied spy fiction and became unexpectedly popular when aired in the United States on Nickelodeon.
Double Dare debuted on Nickelodeon in 1986 and became the network's first original game show, pioneering the messy physical challenge format for kids.
The original Battlestar Galactica aired in 1978 on ABC and was created by Glen A. Larson, inspired partly by the success of Star Wars released the previous year.
The original Battlestar Galactica had a production budget of around $1 million per episode in 1978, making it one of the most expensive TV series ever produced at that time.
The reimagined Battlestar Galactica miniseries aired in 2003 on Sci Fi Channel, developed by Ronald D. Moore, and was praised for its darker, more realistic take on the original series.
In the reimagined BSG, the Cylons were created by humans as robotic servants before they rebelled — a backstory that inverted the original series where Cylons were an alien reptilian race.
Edward James Olmos as Admiral Adama and Mary McDonnell as President Roslin were widely credited with elevating Battlestar Galactica into serious dramatic television territory.
The reimagined Battlestar Galactica used a hand-held camera style and avoided lens flares by policy, giving it a documentary-style realism unusual for science fiction television.
The phrase 'So say we all' became one of the most iconic recurring lines in Battlestar Galactica, used as a collective affirmation throughout the reimagined series.
Battlestar Galactica's reimagined series finale in 2009 drew 2.4 million viewers and was seen as a landmark ending, though its resolution sparked significant fan debate about its final revelations.
The original 1978 Battlestar Galactica spawned a short-lived sequel series called Galactica 1980, which was set on Earth and was widely considered inferior to the original.
Katee Sackhoff's portrayal of Starbuck in the reimagined BSG — changing the character from male to female — became one of the most celebrated gender-swapped reimaginings in sci-fi television.

Knight Rider premiered on NBC in September 1982 and starred David Hasselhoff as Michael Knight, a crime-fighter partnered with KITT — the Knight Industries Two Thousand — an artificially intelligent, nearly indestructible talking car.

KITT in Knight Rider was built on the chassis of a 1982 Pontiac Trans Am; the car's iconic red front scanner light — officially called the Anamorphic Equalizer — was inspired by the Cylon centurion eye sweep from Battlestar Galactica, both shows created by Glen A. Larson.

The voice of KITT in Knight Rider was provided by actor William Daniels, who insisted on remaining uncredited during the show's original run because he feared the role would typecast him; he later embraced the association and reprised the voice in tributes and anniversary specials.

Knight Rider's production team maintained a fleet of over a dozen Pontiac Trans Ams simultaneously — hero cars for close-up dialogue, stunt cars for jumps and crashes, and a special ramp-equipped version for the Turbo Boost sequences.

Knight Rider was created by Glen A. Larson, who conceived it as a modern Lone Ranger — a masked hero with a faithful companion — transplanting the horse into a high-tech car. Larson also created Battlestar Galactica, The Fall Guy, and Magnum P.I.

Knight Rider ran for four seasons from 1982 to 1986 and made David Hasselhoff an international superstar; the show was especially massive in Germany, where Hasselhoff's fame eventually launched a music career that eclipsed his acting work in Europe.

Pontiac reported a significant sales increase in Trans Ams following Knight Rider's premiere, particularly among younger buyers; the show's association with the car is credited with extending the Trans Am's commercial life well into the 1980s.

KITT's canopy in Knight Rider could not actually be opened from the inside during filming — Hasselhoff had to be helped out by crew members between takes, and several scenes required camera angles that concealed the door-opening mechanism.

Knight Rider spawned a 1991 TV movie, a 2000 TV movie, and a 2008 NBC revival series that updated KITT to a Ford Mustang Shelby GT500KR with a morphing nanotechnology body — none matched the original's success or cultural impact.

The Knight Rider theme, composed by Stu Phillips, became one of the most recognizable synthesizer themes of the 1980s; its pulsing, minor-key synth line became shorthand for high-tech cool and was widely imitated in video games and other TV shows of the era.
